items.title: Messy STEAM & Emotions: Sensory Play for Regulation and Discovery
items.summary: An online workshop for trainers who want to use messy, sensory play to connect STEAM exploration with emotional regulation. Participants design activities that support curiosity, calm and safe expression of feelings.
items.participants_countries: Erasmus+ Youth Programme countries , Other countries in the world , Partner Countries Neighbouring the EU
items.description_text: An online workshop for trainers, youth workers and educators who want to intentionally use messy, sensory play as a bridge between STEAM learning and emotional wellbeing. Rather than seeing messy play as “just fun” or “too chaotic”, this training explores how materials like water, colours, clay, sand, foam and simple natural elements (stones, leaves, sticks, soil) can become structured experiences that help young people: explore basic STEAM concepts (mixing, flow, texture, patterns, cause and effect) notice, name and regulate feelings such as curiosity, frustration, excitement, calm or overwhelm. Participants begin by reflecting on their own relationship with mess, control and emotion in their trainer role. Through short prompts and sharing, they identify situations where learners’ emotions (boredom, anxiety, hyper‑energy, shutdown) make facilitation challenging – and where sensory experiences could support regulation instead of adding more stress. The workshop then introduces key principles of sensory‑rich STEAM activities: starting from simple questions (“What happens when…?”, “How does it feel when…?”) offering choices of materials and levels of mess (dry vs wet, smooth vs rough, colour vs neutral) scaffolding from free exploration to small challenges (e.g. build, mix, separate, transform) integrating reflection moments that connect body sensations, emotions and observations. Using these principles, participants design 1–2 messy STEAM activities suitable for their contexts – for example: water + colour + different containers for exploring flow and intensity, linked to talking about strong vs gentle emotions clay or dough for shaping “emotional creatures” or “problem shapes”, followed by discussion on what they represent nature trays with stones, leaves and sand to build “islands of calm” or “stormy landscapes”, connecting to stress and coping strategies. For each activity, they define: the STEAM focus (e.g. mixing, states of matter, patterns, structure) the emoti

items.title: Play to Learn: Creating Educational Games for Youth Work
items.summary: A creative online workshop for youth workers who want to turn real issues from their practice into simple learning games using scarves, balls and easy, nature‑found materials. Each new game becomes a new question to explore with young people.
items.participants_countries: Erasmus+ Youth Programme countries , Other countries in the world , Partner Countries Neighbouring the EU
items.description_text: An online workshop for youth workers, trainers and educators who want to move beyond using ready‑made games and start designing their own educational games around real themes from their practice. Every game is treated as a new question: an invitation for young people to explore “What is really happening here?” and “What could we do differently?” through play. The focus is on low‑tech, easy‑to‑run games that can be prototyped and facilitated with very simple materials: scarves, balls and objects that participants can either create themselves or find easily in nature or their immediate environment (stones, sticks, leaves, paper, recycled items). This makes the method realistic for any youth setting, regardless of budget or equipment. Participants are invited to bring one concrete issue or learning goal from their context – for example: low participation in discussions, online conflict, inclusion and exclusion, stress and wellbeing, or ethical questions around AI and technology. Through guided reflection, they translate this into a potential game situation: Who are the players? What choices do they face? What tensions or dilemmas should appear in the game? What question will this game ask the group? The workshop introduces the core elements of game design for learning: a clear learning purpose and target group a simple narrative or theme that makes sense for young people roles, rules and resources (such as scarves, balls, natural objects) game mechanics – what players actually do and repeat during play feedback and endings – how a round finishes and what “success” can look like debriefing questions that help participants connect their experience back to the central question behind the game. In small design teams or individually, participants sketch a first analogue prototype using whatever they have at hand: drawing a space or path on paper, assigning meanings to different coloured scarves or types of balls, or using found/natural materials as tokens, obstacles or reso

items.title: Outdoor Paths: Designing Experiential Activities for Young People
items.summary: An online workshop for youth workers to design safe, inclusive outdoor learning journeys. Using visual “island–storm–harbour” maps, participants turn real group needs into structured outdoor games, reflection walks and community‑based challenges.
items.participants_countries: Erasmus+ Youth Programme countries , Other countries in the world , Partner Countries Neighbouring the EU
items.description_text: This online workshop is designed for youth workers who already use games and activities with young people and want to upgrade them into well‑designed outdoor and experiential learning processes. Instead of offering a random collection of icebreakers, the training focuses on how to: turn real issues from youth work (group tension, low participation, stress, intercultural challenges) into outdoor and experiential activities plan activities that are safe, inclusive and purposeful in very different local contexts – from small neighbourhood parks to busy city centres. Participants work with a simple “learning journey” metaphor (island – wind – storms – harbour – legacy) to map what supports and what blocks them when they plan outdoor learning with young people. Building on this, they learn a step‑by‑step planning model: Define a clear learning focus (e.g. trust, communication, dealing with uncertainty, reconnecting with nature). Choose and analyse a real space they have access to (street, park, school yard, seaside, village square). Design an activity that uses this space as a co‑facilitator: routes, objects, landmarks and people become part of the game or challenge. Prepare guiding questions for briefing, risk assessment, inclusion (who might be left out and why), and debriefing. The workshop mixes short inputs with practical design sprints in small groups. Participants leave with one concrete outdoor / experiential activity plan, ready to test with their group, plus a checklist for adapting it to different weather, group sizes and access needs.

items.title: Robotics in Youth Work: From Playful Missions to Real Skills
items.summary: An online workshop using simple examples and mission‑based design, participants learn how to use robots to develop teamwork, problem‑solving and creativity with young people – even with limited equipment.
items.participants_countries: Erasmus+ Youth Programme countries , Other countries in the world , Partner Countries Neighbouring the EU
items.description_text: A practical online workshop designed for youth workers, trainers and educators who want to explore educational robotics as a tool for learning. The focus is on how robots and simple programmable devices can support skills such as collaboration, planning, resilience and systems thinking. Participants explore what “robotics” can look like in youth work: from programmable toys and simple kits to low‑tech simulations of robots when no hardware is available. Through concrete examples, the session introduces the idea of mission‑based activities: small challenges where a “robot” has to achieve a goal (follow a line, move an object, navigate a map, simulate a social scenario). Participants see how each mission can be framed around real‑life issues, such as accessibility, environmental protection, or community problem‑solving, turning robotics into a vehicle for values and discussion. In groups, participants design one robotics session tailored to their own context and resources. They choose a mission, define roles within the team (e.g. programmer, builder, tester, documenter), and outline the steps from briefing to testing to debriefing. They also plan how they will handle mistakes and failures as learning moments, and how they will invite all young people to contribute, regardless of technical confidence. The workshop concludes with presentations of the session plans, peer feedback and a short reflection on how robotics can connect to broader youth work goals, such as inclusion, participation and digital literacy. By the end of the workshop, participants will: see robotics as an accessible method for youth work rather than a purely technical field understand how to frame robotics activities as missions with clear learning goals have a ready‑to‑test session plan adapted to their own level of equipment and group needs feel more prepared to use robotics to support soft skills, creativity and interest in technology among young people.

items.title: STEAM in Youth Work: Creative projects for young people
items.summary: An introductory online workshop for youth workers to use STEAM (Science, Technology, Engineering, Arts, Maths) in non‑formal education. Participants design simple, low‑threshold STEAM activities that foster creativity, teamwork and critical thinking.
items.participants_countries: Erasmus+ Youth Programme countries , Other countries in the world , Partner Countries Neighbouring the EU
items.fee_text: The participation fee is set as a symbolic contribution of 15€.
items.description_text: Online workshop for youth workers, trainers and educators who want to bring Science, Technology, Engineering, Arts and Mathematics into their practice in a playful way. The workshop is as a toolbox for creativity, problem‑solving and inclusion in non‑formal learning. Participants begin by mapping the realities of their groups: where their young people are curious, where they feel excluded from “science”, and what resources are actually available in their local context. Using simple visual tools and collective brainstorming, they identify everyday themes (community, environment, digital life, culture) that can become STEAM projects without the need for expensive equipment. The workshop then introduces key principles of STEAM in youth work: hands‑on exploration, connecting arts and creativity with logic and making, linking projects to real‑life issues and allowing space for trial and error. Through examples and mini‑demos, participants see how simple materials (paper, recycled objects, basic digital tools) can be used to create small experiments, prototypes or installations that engage diverse learning styles. In small groups, participants design their own 60–90 minute STEAM activity tailored to their context. They define the learning goals, describe the step‑by‑step process, list materials and roles, and plan how they will support reflection and debriefing with the young people. Special attention is given to inclusion: how to ensure that young people with different backgrounds, abilities or language levels can participate meaningfully. The session closes with a sharing round, where participants present their activity ideas, give and receive feedback, and identify possible next steps (e.g. testing with their group, adapting for online/hybrid use, integrating into a project or exchange). By the end of the workshop, participants will: understand what STEAM can mean in a non‑formal youth work setting be able to identify opportunities for STEAM‑based activities in their o

items.title: Ask Better, Teach Better: Question‑driven AI & advocacy in youth work
items.summary: A hands-on online workshop for youth workers to design better questions – for their groups and for AI. Using empathy maps, fishbone and “How might we…?” prompts, participants turn real needs into meaningful learning and concrete actions.
items.participants_countries: Erasmus+ Youth Programme countries , Other countries in the world , Partner Countries Neighbouring the EU
items.fee_text: The participation fee is set as a symbolic contribution of 10€.
items.description_text: Artificial Intelligence and digital tools are becoming part of the everyday reality of young people. They use search engines, recommendation systems and generative AI to ask questions, look for answers and create content. Youth workers and educators are expected to guide them in this new landscape, often without feeling properly equipped themselves. At the same time, the heart of quality youth work is still the same: listening carefully, asking meaningful questions, and turning lived experiences into collective learning and action. This online workshop brings these two dimensions together. Instead of focusing on “AI tricks”, it focuses on the art of the question. Participants will work with visual, non‑formal tools such as empathy maps, fishbone diagrams and impact/feasibility matrices to explore the real needs and root causes behind their everyday challenges. From there, they will create strong “How might we…?” questions that are specific, open, positive and human‑centred – and then test how both human peers and AI tools can help them move from good questions to concrete next steps (resources, proposals, campaigns, policy suggestions, etc.). The main aim of the workshop is to support youth workers and trainers to design questions that: start from real stories and needs in their youth work practice, use AI in a critical and purposeful way, and lead towards meaningful learning and tangible action in and with their communities. During the workshop, participants will: map their own motivations, expectations and possible “storms” around AI and questioning through a short journey‑style exercise (island, wind, storms, harbour, legacy); use tools such as an empathy map and a fishbone diagram to explore the root causes of one concrete challenge from their context; transform this analysis into one or more “How might we…?” questions that meet key criteria for good questions (connected to a real need, specific enough, open enough, positive, human‑centred); practice formulating

items.description_text: Cohesive Youth Work – Austria Cohesive Youth Work (CYW) is an Erasmus+ international training course designed for professionals and volunteers active in non-formal education and youth work. The project brings together participants from Austria, Italy, Portugal, Romania, Hungary, and Slovakia to explore how youth work can build trust, dialogue, participation, and community cohesion. Integration Phase (November 2026 – February 2027) Apply what you learn in your own community by identifying local needs and strengths, participating in online mentoring, and preparing your own community initiative. Phase 2 – Vienna (22–27 February 2027) Transform your ideas into action by learning participatory project design, stakeholder engagement, and practical tools to develop a local Cohesion Action for your community. We are looking for 5 participants who: are 18+ and currently live in Austria or Slovakia ; are actively involved in youth work or non-formal education (NGOs, youth centres, schools, municipalities, community initiatives, volunteering, etc.); have a good command of English; are motivated to participate in both training courses and implement a local follow-up activity. We particularly encourage applications from youth workers, volunteers, educators, and community leaders from rural and smaller municipalities across Austria. items.title: VR/AR - From Questions to Worlds: Interactive Story + Analog Game + AI Video Events
items.summary: Turn real issues from their youth work into interactive stories and low‑tech games. Using branching narratives, analog play and short AI‑generated videos, participants design questions that open dialogue and action.
items.participants_countries: Erasmus+ Youth Programme countries , Other countries in the world , Partner Countries Neighbouring the EU
items.description_text: An experiential online lab for trainers and youth workers that combines the spirit of Ask Better, Teach Better with the creative tools of Play to Learn. The focus is on how powerful questions can be turned into interactive stories, low‑tech games and short AI‑generated video events that help young people explore complex situations in a safe, playful way. Participants start from their own practice: each small group brings one concrete issue from their work with young people (for example participation, conflict, online behaviour, inclusion, or the impact of AI). They turn this into a single “How might we…?” question which becomes the core of the activity. Building on this, they design a very simple branching story in 3–4 scenes, where different types of questions and choices lead to different outcomes. This can be done with basic digital tools or even on paper, so the method stays accessible. The next step is to translate the story into a physical game. Groups create a simple shared space or board, define roles and a common goal, and design question cards and event cards that players draw as they move through the situation. The game is intentionally low‑tech, using materials that are easy to find in any youth setting (paper, markers, scarves, balls, simple objects), to show that meaningful game design does not require expensive equipment. As an additional layer, participants experiment with short AI‑generated videos to “bring to life” 2–3 key events from their story. These videos are used as live triggers during the game: when an AI‑video event appears, the group pauses, watches, and must first describe what they see and formulate new questions before deciding how to act in the game. In this way, AI is framed as a prompt for reflection and questioning, not as a source of ready‑made answers. Throughout the workshop, attention is given to inclusion, emotional safety and ethics around using AI‑generated media. The session closes with reflection on how each game functions

Young people are exposed to this world even more, as they are „digital natives“. This training corse was created as iniciative in the beginning of Covid-19 pandemic as national training for teachers, educators and youthworkers in Slovakia. So far more than 15 trainings were carried out in 2 years period. During implementation of this training we found out that there is a need to provide similar format of training course in english so wider group of participants can join. Thus networking activity ON/OFF was created. This training will offer insight into vast world of online threats and opportunities that anyone can use and learn about and will offer youthworkers and educators from different contries important conterporary skills, that are useful during online trainings and education, but are useful in offline evnironment as well. Main goals of the training course: · show different online tools and their functionality (tools like Kahoot, Mentimeter, Prezi, Google Suite, Canva, Adobe Expres, Miro and lot more). · show synergies and crossfunctionality of various tools, how to use them together, how they can replace

items.description_text: Green Skills 4 Future is a two-stage international Erasmus+ training course designed for youth workers, educators, trainers, and motivated individuals interested in sustainability and green skills. The project aims to strengthen participants’ understanding of the green economy, climate challenges, and sustainable practices while providing practical tools to engage young people in environmental action. Through interactive workshops, DIY activities, study visits, and hands-on learning experiences, participants will explore eco-friendly solutions and learn how to promote sustainable lifestyles within their communities. The first stage takes place in Bulgaria (15–22 October 2026) and introduces key concepts of sustainability and green opportunities. The second stage, held in the Czech Republic (23–30 April 2027), deepens learning through practical activities and collaboration with local youth. The project supports the development of green competencies, encourages active citizenship, and empowers participants to become ambassadors of positive environmental change.

items.description_text: GreenSport 2030 is a European cooperation project under the Erasmus+ Sport programme that supports grassroots sport organisations in transitioning towards more sustainable, digitally enabled, and inclusive practices. The project introduces the GreenSport Hub, an AI-powered platform that allows sport organisations to monitor environmental impact, including energy use, waste, and carbon footprint, and to improve their performance through data-driven insights. In parallel, the project delivers capacity-building activities for youth oriented professions and promotes circular economy solutions such as equipment reuse, and facilitates policy dialogue to integrate sustainability criteria into sport governance and funding mechanisms. By combining innovation, education, and policy engagement, GreenSport 2030 contributes to EU priorities on climate action, digital transformation, and social inclusion in sport This webinar is part of series of dissemination activities and explores effective communication strategies, stakeholder engagement, and awareness campaigns at local and EU level that can be sued by youth workers and trainers using sport as a tool in their daily work.

items.description_text: Discover the potential of Youth Exchanges In 3 training days, we show you the possibilities Erasmus+ Youth Exchanges have to offer for young people from disadvantaged backgrounds. An international youth project might just be the life-changing opportunity for the disadvantaged young people you are working with: e.g. those from minority backgrounds, with special needs, with socio-economic difficulties, discriminated groups, in difficult situations, with limited opportunities... Why participate? International Youth Exchanges are a rewarding methodology to reach your organisation’s inclusion aims. This Youth Exchanges for ALL course gives you concrete examples of what is possible, brings you in contact with other inclusion youth organisations (from Europe and EuroMed), shows you what funding is available specifically for youth inclusion projects and walks you step-by-step through the process in case you would like to give it a try. At the end of the course, you will be ready to start organising a youth exchange with/by/for the young people with fewer opportunities in your organisation. Who is the course for? This course is for NEWCOMERS to Erasmus+ Youth (e.g. youth worker, social worker,...) who are directly working with young people from disadvantaged backgrounds (e.g. facing social, economic, educational, geographic, cultural, health & disability challenges). If you NEVER organised an international youth project with your target group before and would like to know what is possible, then this course is for you! If you already organised mobility projects for young people, please check the European Training Calendar for a more appropriate youth work training because this course is for NEWCOMERS ;-) This course is NOT for school-related activities such as school exchanges or projects for pupils (please contact the Lifelong Learning colleagues of Erasmus+ for this). This course welcomes candidates with special needs (e.g. health conditions, disability, single mothers,...)

items.description_text: We invite youth workers from Programme countries and Southern Mediterranean countries to take part in an international Partnership Building Activity (PBA) in Northern Portugal focused on inclusive youth work with young women, particularly those living in rural, remote or peripheral communities. Young women living in rural and peripheral areas experience unique realities that present both opportunities and challenges. Rural communities often offer strong social connections, a sense of belonging and growing spaces for community engagement, local leadership and social innovation. At the same time, young women may face barriers such as geographical isolation, limited mobility, reduced access to information, fewer educational and professional opportunities, persistent gender stereotypes and less direct access to youth services and international experiences. These factors can particularly affect young women with fewer opportunities, influencing their participation in community life and their awareness of, and access to, local, national and European opportunities. Youth work has an important role to play in recognising the strengths of rural communities while addressing the barriers that prevent young women from fully participating, accessing information and benefiting from the opportunities available to them. Youth workers and youth organisations play an important role in addressing these inequalities. They can create inclusive spaces, provide information, accompany young women in accessing opportunities and develop practices that respond to the realities of their communities. The PBA will bring together youth workers from E+ and ESC Programme and Southern Mediterranean countries to exchange experiences and reflect on how their organisations engage young women, remove barriers and make opportunities more visible, accessible and inclusive. The programme will include a visit to a local organisation in Northern Portugal working with young women or rural communities. This act

items.description_text: The Council of Europe Youth Department, in the framework of the project Youth revitalising democracy, and the Democracy Reloading Partnership of Erasmus+ national agencies are organising a joint Seminar for municipal youth policy practitioners. The seminar will take place in the European Youth Center Budapest, Hungary, on 16-21 November 2026 bringing together 50 participants. The activity aims to support local and regional municipalities in designing and implementing participative youth policies in line with the new European Charter on the Participation of Young People in Local and Regional Life. The Charter is the key standard for youth participation at local and regional level. Adopted in 1992 for the first time, it was subject of a thorough revision in 2024-2025 to which more than 400 stakeholders participated, being adopted by the Congress of Local and Regional Authorities and the Committee of Ministers of the Council of Europe in 2026. The Council of Europe and its partners at international, national and local level are taking measures to secure the implementation of the Charter provisions. Expected profile of participants of the seminar: Municipal youth policy officers and their partners, especially local youth councils or youth civil society, engaged in planning and implementing local or regional youth participation policy and practice Priority will be given to participants from the partner countries of the Democracy Reloading Partnership, but not exclusively. items.description_text: Background & Context Şanlıurfa sits at a historical and geographic crossroads. As one of the cities hosting the highest populations of displaced persons and migrants in the region, its local ecosystem has accumulated extensive, hard-earned expertise in crisis response, social cohesion, and the integration of migrant youth. This Study Visit is designed to move beyond theoretical frameworks and provide an unfiltered, on-the-ground look at how local NGOs, youth centers and municipal bodies navigate the complexities of inclusion. Participants will observe the direct application of non-formal education methodologies and youth work practices in high-stakes environments. Objectives of the Study Visit Field Observation: To explore successful and challenging practices in the social, educational and economic inclusion of migrant youth in Şanlıurfa. Methodological Exchange: To analyze specific NFE tools and youth work approaches utilized by local practitioners to bridge cultural divides and foster community resilience. Ecosystem Mapping: To understand the coordination between different stakeholders, including local government, civil society organizations and international agencies operating in the region. Partnership Building: To facilitate networking among participants, serving as a catalyst for future joint projects under the Erasmus+ and European Solidarity Corps (ESC) programs focusing on inclusion and diversity. Draft Program Structure The working methods will heavily rely on active participation, peer-to-peer learning, and critical reflection. The agenda will include: Visits to local youth centers and NGOs operating directly in migrant neighborhoods. Dialogue sessions with field workers, project coordinators and the target youth groups. Thematic workshops dissecting the transferability of local practices to other European contexts. Dedicated partnership-building blocks for future Erasmus+/ESC initiatives. The training will combine elements of: self-reflection (how do youth workers tackle the topic) building youth workers’ confidence in facilitating discussions about war and armed conflicts with young people exchanging experiences of dealing with difficult conversations with young people on war related topics equipping youth workers with methods and practical tools to help discuss difficult topics with young people opportunities in Erasmus+ and European Solidarity Corps possibilities for youth workers and young people on the topic if feasible, a study visit to a Viennese organisation will be incorporated Methodology of the training course: The methodology of the training course is based on the principles and methods of non-formal education and experiential learning. items.description_text: Across Europe, democratic values are increasingly under pressure, and public discourse is becoming more polarised. Amid these developments, youth work stands at a crossroads: socially and politically relevant enough to matter yet increasingly expected to remain “apolitical” and focus on individual life skills. Youth work practitioners often find themselves navigating difficult dilemmas, such as how youth work can operate effectively in politically volatile and polarised environments, what its role is in safeguarding democracy, and whether depoliticisation is a viable response or itself a political choice. And if not, how can youth workers navigate the risks and responsibilities of taking a stand for democracy? The seminar targets youth work practitioners who want to strengthen democratic values and attitudes amongst their target group and within their communities, and exchange ideas and experiences with like-minded colleagues. In particular, the seminar aims to offer a space for youth work practitioners to: -explore the current state of democracy and better understand its implications for youth work and young people's lives; -reflect on the ethical and political dimensions of youth work and its role in promoting and sustaining democratic values; -examine existing projects, initiatives, and practices that have responded to democratic backsliding and critically reflect on what worked well and what was less effective; -enable and support participants in finding the right approach in their youth work to strengthen and support democratic values in their local context; -encourage participants to develop concrete follow-up activities for their local youth work and communities. (Guidance and advice will be provided by the seminar facilitators.); -build a supportive network of youth work practitioners committed to democracy, enabling continued exchange, peer support, and cooperation beyond the seminar itself. items.description_text: Youth Work Can Promote Mental Health The Mental Health in Youth Work training explores the role and competencies of the youth workers in supporting the mental health needs of young people by strengthening their emotional and mental health skills. Objectives The training course objectives are: Clarifying the understanding of what mental health means Understanding the role of youth workers in promoting mental health in their work with young people Developing youth workers’ practical skills in supporting people’s mental health in their work Encouraging participants to reflect on their everyday practices with young people Enhancing the capacity to create a supportive, inclusive, and stigma-free environment Mental Health from a preventive perspective This training highlights mental health from a promotion and prevention perspective. In this training, we do not approach mental health from the point of view of diagnosis, therapy, or healing – these approaches belong to the professional work outside youth work practices and require a set of competences that youth workers do not need to have. Who can participate? items.description_text: This ID Talks series will look into the concept of social sport - what it means, why it is important and how it promotes inclusion, social cohesion and skills development. Introduction Social sport is applied in various settings, including youth work, education and community development. It is a powerful learning tool that addresses social challenges and creates positive change. Social sport boosts inclusion and builds real-life skills. It strengthens cooperation, respect, health and active citizenship. And it often reaches target groups that other methods don’t. In autumn 2026, the SALTO Inclusion & Diversity Resources Centre partners up with the Erasmus+ National Agencies’ YouSports project to contribute to the discussion on sport as an inclusive non-formal educational tool for personal and community impact. How can youth work use social sport to reach out to underrepresented groups, ensuring their inclusion? In the YouSports project, social sport is understood in the following ways: as an educational tool for added value, with a focus on the experience, as well as winning, engaging and fun, reflecting on the learning, transfer competences to other real-life situations, as a process for individual development and community impact. We will look into practices of education through sport that contribute to inclusive communities and nourish the wellbeing of young people with fewer opportunities. The 5 online “ID Talks” promote inclusion & diversity (ID) and quality youth work. Youth workers, professionals and volunteers from all over the world share insights, research findings, food for thought, good practices or inspirational stories. They guide participants through the pressing matters and challenges affecting inclusion & diversity and inspire them to make their programmes and organisations more inclusive, embrace human differences, look ahead and picture how the future of youth work and inclusion &diversity might be. items.description_text: Facilating a group is about much more than leading activities or collecting methods. Behind every learning experience lies a series of choices: how we create a safe and inclusive environment, how we support individual and group learning processes, and how we ensure that participants can transfer what they learn into their everyday lives. This training course is designed for youth workers, trainers, educators, youth leaders and other professionals active in the field of non-formal learning who want to strengthen their facilitation skills and deepen their understanding of meaningful educational work. Through a combination of practice, reflection and experiential learning, participants will explore the role of the facilitator and discover how to design learning experiences that are engaging, inclusive and impactful. The course will offer practical tools and methodologies from non-formal education while also creating space to reflect on personal facilitation style, values and educational purpose. Participants can expect a diverse learning experience that combines theory, experiential learning, group processes and creative approaches. The training will draw on established frameworks for quality learning and competence development while remaining grounded in the realities of youth work and community education. Topics explored during the course include: • the role and responsibilities of a facilitator; • non-formal education methodologies and learning design; • creating safe, inclusive and participatory learning environments; • supporting individual and group learning processes; • designing educational experiences with lasting impact; • developing a personal facilitation style; • building partnerships and cooperation among youth workers and organisations. The course will also provide opportunities to exchange practices, learn from peers across Europe and build meaningful professional connections. items.description_text: Young people who are not in education, employment or training (NEET) face diverse and often interconnected barriers to social inclusion. This seminar aims to strengthen the competences of youth workers by exploring the challenges faced by NEET youth, sharing good practices and discussing practical approaches to outreach, engagement and empowerment. After this seminar, you will gain a better understanding of the NEET demographic, learn about intervention strategies and have more resources to address NEET-related issues. What is this activity? This activity is part of a Training and Cooperation Activity (TCA) organized by the Turkish National Agency for Erasmus+ & ESC. TCA is a tool for National Agencies to support the Erasmus+ Program and enhance its impact. Travel costs and accommodation is covered. For details please check the "Costs" section which is located on the right side of this page. Objectives: To deepen participants' understanding of the needs and challenges of NEET youth. To explore effective engagement strategies for reaching young people with fewer opportunities. To exchange good practices, tools and experiences from different national and organisational contexts. To identify pathways that support the social inclusion, employability and active participation of NEET youth. Target Group: Youth workers and youth leaders, Youth researchers, Social workers, representatives of social services, Local authorities/policy makers responsible for social provision Project managers of all experience levels, interested in NEET youth. Methodology: The seminar will combine expert presentations, panel discussions and peer exchange to encourage reflection and the sharing of experiences. Interactive sessions will provide participants with practical insights, good practices and opportunities to discuss challenges and approaches to working with NEET youth in different contexts.

items.description_text: Grow is an international training course, where youth workers will be introduced to essential tools for personal development, grounded in self-awareness and cognitive growth. Through a mix of reflective exercises, coaching techniques, and empathic communication practices, participants will deepen their understanding of their own strengths and thought patterns. This project builds a strong foundation for leadership by enhancing emotional intelligence and fostering peace-building attitudes. The program emphasizes self-reflection as the basis for sustainable development and prepares youth workers to guide others effectively.

items.description_text: Das Training richtet sich an Fachkräfte der Jugendarbeit sowie an Entscheidungsträgerinnen und Entscheidungsträger, die sich mit der Gestaltung, Entwicklung und politischen Rahmung von Räumen für junge Menschen befassen. Ziel des Trainings ist es, neue Perspektiven auf jugendgerechte Orte zu eröffnen und innovative Modelle kennenzulernen, die Begegnung, Austausch und selbstbestimmte Nutzung ermöglichen. Ausgangspunkt bildet der in Luxemburg formulierte Bedarf junger Menschen nach frei zugänglichen, sicheren und attraktiven Orten, an denen sie sich treffen, Zeit miteinander verbringen, Erlebnisse teilen und Erfahrungen sammeln können. Das Training greift diesen Bedarf auf und lädt dazu ein, das Angebot an Räumen für Jugendliche neu zu denken und weiterzuentwickeln. Im Rahmen des Trainings werden europäische Praxisbeispiele aus Belgien, Südtirol, der Schweiz und Deutschland vorgestellt. Sie zeigen, wie Räume für junge Menschen in unterschiedlichen Kontexten gestaltet und betrieben werden können. Ergänzend werden die luxemburgischen Rahmenbedingungen beleuchtet. Die Teilnehmenden haben die Möglichkeit, sich mit Vertreterinnen und Vertretern der vorgestellten Organisationen auszutauschen und Einblicke in deren Arbeitsweisen, Herausforderungen und Erfolgsfaktoren zu gewinnen. Ein weiterer Programmpunkt ist die Besichtigung eines vom nationalen Jugenddienst geführten Maker Space. Die Teilnehmenden lernen vor Ort ein konkretes Praxisbeispiel kennen und können sich mit den verantwortlichen Fachkräften austauschen. Das Training bietet Raum für Reflexion, Diskussion und Vernetzung. Es unterstützt die Teilnehmenden dabei, neue Ansätze kennenzulernen und Anregungen für die eigene Praxis mitzunehmen. Ziel ist es, Impulse für die Weiterentwicklung jugendgerechter Räume in Europa zu setzen und Angebote nachhaltig an den Bedürfnissen junger Menschen auszurichten.

items.description_text: Are you a youth leader or youth worker committed to becoming an agent of change in your local community and beyond? Are you interested in learning more about what quality youth participation means and how you can embed it in your organisational life or the new projects that you plan? Do you want to explore the opportunities of the Erasmus+ Programme? Then “Dive into Youth Participation” is the Training Course for you! Youth participation in democratic life and young people's active civic engagement are high priorities of the EU Youth Strategy and are incorporated into the majority of contemporary youth policy frameworks in different European countries. The Erasmus+ Programme is one of the mechanisms to put the EU Youth Strategy into practice and provide many opportunities for young people to develop international projects, which empower them to take an active role in society and help them develop civic and social competencies. In this context, the fourth edition of the Training Course “Dive into Youth Participation” brings together 25 youth leaders and youth workers from different European countries with an aim to improve their understanding and practices toward quality youth participation. This is also an opportunity to learn more about the possibilities within the Erasmus+ Key Action 154 “Youth Participation Activities” and how to ensure youth participation is properly implemented in all stages of a project or participation initiative. Profile of participants The call is open for youth leaders and youth workers who fit the following criteria: -representatives of youth/youth work organisations or informal groups of young people; -have experience with youth participation or civic engagement; -are interested in developing projects within the Erasmus+ Key Action 154 “Youth Participation Activities” (no prior experience is required); -are interested in developing/improving youth participation practices/projects at any level (local, national, European or international)

items.description_text: This course is a self-paced, interactive, practical course for Youth Workers to learn how to support groups of youth with fewer/less opportunities in creating and realizing their own volunteering projects and use it as an experiential learning process for the volunteers. It is a Beta version, please provide your feedback on the feedback forms incorparated into the course, to help us make it as good and useful as possible! You are going to learn about: - how to approach youth with fewer/less opportunities, - how to mobilize and recruit them to create their own volunteer projects, - how to work with them, - how to inspire, connect, empower, coach and mentor them in this process. About this course: In this course, you have a chance to learn about supporting youth with fewer/less opportunities who come from minority or socially disadvantaged background in the context of youth work. More specifically you can learn about how to support a group of youth with fewer/less opportunities in a non-formal learning program that is based on volunteering. The above-mentioned topics are explored by sharing and expanding a field-tested practice of working with Hungarian youth living in foster care and in segregated minority communities run since 2011. This method is called: From Us to You (FUY/FUYI - but we like to pronounce it FUJI). Aims and objectives of the course: The course aims to introduce the elements of the From Us to You program that make a volunteering based non-formal education program effective. By doing so we would like to share what we have found useful in our work in supporting youth with fewer/less opportunities. Besides our learnings, we also share the resources and approaches we have learned from within or outside of the field of youth work. More concretely, you will have a short glimpse into: - Experiential education - Solution focused approach - Trauma informed approach. items.description_text: How can international youth mobility become truly safe, inclusive, and empowering for Highly Vulnerable Young People? A course for youth workers, facilitators, and organisations to learn how to create safer, more inclusive, and trauma-informed international mobility experiences for Highly Vulnerable Young People (HVYP). This is a self-paced and practice-oriented online course for professionals working in youth mobility and non-formal education. The course supports participants in developing the competencies needed to responsibly include and support young people facing multiple and intersecting barriers in international programmes. You are going to learn about: safer and more inclusive mobility environments, safeguarding and risk management in practice, trauma-informed and solution-focused approaches, recognising and preventing harm and exclusion, supporting HVYP before, during, and after mobility experiences, professional boundaries, crisis response, and self-care in youth work. About this course International youth mobility projects can create powerful opportunities for learning, participation, connection, and personal growth. At the same time, for highly vulnerable young people, unfamiliar international environments may also involve increased vulnerability and risk. This course was developed within the LADDER project to support youth workers and organisations in creating mobility experiences that are not only accessible, but genuinely safe, supportive, and empowering. Through practical examples, case studies, reflection exercises, and field-tested approaches, participants will strengthen their capacity to: create safer and more inclusive mobility projects, anticipate and prevent risks, respond responsibly to challenging situations, and support young people facing multiple barriers. items.description_text: The project arises from a shared observation among young people, community development professionals, and partner organizations: a growing need to reconnect with the living world, rebuild social bonds, and collectively reflect on current ecological challenges in the face of the climate emergency. It addresses two main needs: Strengthening the skills of facilitators and community leaders so they can design and implement educational projects with strong social and ecological impact. Actively engaging young people in meaningful, concrete, and transformative projects. The project uses the living garden as an educational, sensory, and cooperative tool, helping to recreate connections between humans and ecosystems. These spaces become platforms for social transformation, civic education, and territorial resilience. It is based on active pedagogy that promotes autonomy, local engagement, and the transversal skills required for the ecological transition. The event will take place outdoors and is aimed at educators, activity leaders, and facilitators. In Brittany, 27 participants are expected.
